The Art of Text Communication Every Software Engineer Should Learn
A few words don't sound like much, but how you write them changes how they land — and for software engineers, who communicate mostly through text (Slack, Discord, email), this is a real skill.
At work and at home, we communicate constantly through chat tools — Slack, Messenger, Discord, email. In real life,
matters — but in text, that art doesn't translate directly. Because

You've probably had this happen — you sent a message that meant one thing, the other person read it as something completely different, and now there's a fight. Software engineers especially need to be careful — one tone-deaf phrasing to your manager and it can spiral. There's a "text art" that mirrors verbal communication skill. The rest of this post covers the parts that trip people up.

When we talk face to face, we add tone, body language, expression, eye contact — all the signals that disambiguate the message. None of that survives the trip from your fingers to someone else's screen. Imagine Mark messages the team's lunch group: "We're going to
What's Mark actually saying? Two possible reads:
whether everyone wants to go to that place
It's A — he's asking. Did you read it as A? Maybe. But to plenty of readers, B feels just as plausible. Text leaves room for that.
Another scenario — a real example from a friend. Friend's boss messages something like:
A natural reading is: "If you can't finish this today, you're fired. Any questions?" Friend panicked.

Actual intent — "If this feature isn't done today, just go home on time. Don't stay late." Without tone, totally invisible. And even if you reply asking what they meant, the other person isn't always around to clarify right away — so you stew. Sounds dramatic, but it actually happens.
Things that change how text lands
Don't use abbreviations only you understand
Stacking punctuation has the opposite effect of what you'd think.
"If you can't finish today just leave!!" — those exclamation marks aren't softening the message, they're sharpening it. The
is for emphasis, and stacking it makes everything feel intensified, which often comes across as aggression.

Same with question marks. "Why didn't you?" lands different from "Why didn't you???" — the second one feels confrontational. Repeated punctuation is high-volume. Use it deliberately, and only when you actually want the energy.

There's a different case where multiple punctuation makes sense — when you're communicating genuine surprise. "I can ride a bike super well!!!!!!!" is excessive. But "I just saw a UFO!!!!!!!" — sure, that probably warrants the energy.

Don't compress aggressively unless you're sure the other person reads it the same way. Excessive shorthand misfires constantly — unless you know this person well enough that you basically read their mind.

The boss example earlier — "if you can't finish today just leave" was their compression of "if it isn't done today, go home and don't stay late". Reading the compressed version, the listener filled in the worst possible interpretation. Same pattern, every team.
Modal particles are the little words that signal tone — "yeah", "ok", "k", "kk", "alright", "sure", "got it". In English, the same content reads totally different depending on which one you use.

These are all "I acknowledge what you said" — but the vibe shifts. "ok" is neutral. "k" can read as terse / dismissive. "ok." (with a period) can feel cold. "ok!" is friendly. "okay" sounds more invested than "ok". Tiny choices, big perception shifts.

The Mandarin original of this article spends a lot of time on Chinese-specific particles like 喔 / 嗯 / 嗯嗯 vs 恩恩, which don't translate directly. If you're communicating in Mandarin, the original Mandarin version of this post has more detail on those specifically.
Emojis and internet slang are their own field. Knowing what each emoji means matters — using a mismatched emoji is worse than using none.
— in my opinion / in my humble opinion
No emoji has a universal meaning — but knowing roughly how each lands helps you not get misread.
Pair text with an emoji or sticker

If text shorthand isn't your strong suit, you can add an emoji or sticker at the end of a sentence to signal tone. Want to actually express enthusiasm about that restaurant? Pair the request with an enthusiastic emoji. Don't pair it with something unrelated — that confuses the message rather than clarifying it:

Text communication is a real skill — easy to dismiss because the words look simple, but they aren't. Getting a sentence to land correctly takes attention to word choice, punctuation, modal particles, and emojis as a system. Even the difference between "ok", "ok!", and "ok?" carries information.
Now think about it with punctuation or emojis attached:

Same word, four different messages. That's the power of text combined with intent markers. Spend a beat reading your message back from the recipient's perspective — that one beat catches most miscommunications.

A few words don't sound like much, but the way you write them changes how they land — and for software engineers, who do most of their communication through text (Slack, Discord, email), this matters a lot. This post covers what trips people up most in text conversations: over-punctuation, private abbreviations, modal particles, and emojis. Examples include a real boss-misread case from a friend, and the small adjustments that shift tone without rewriting your sentence.
